metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the git-cliff invocation mode in the runbook step and cliff.toml notes: use `git-cliff --unreleased --prepend CHANGELOG.md` (prepend mode) so only new commits are added at the top and curated sections are never overwritten; update AC2 to scope determinism to the `--unreleased` run ('running git-cliff --unreleased twice produces no diff') rather than implying full-file idempotency post-curation.
findings.finding: The plan's generate-then-curate mechanism has a hidden overwrite ordering hazard: git-cliff's default invocation rewrites the entire CHANGELOG.md from commit history, so any re-run after curation (e.g., at the next release cycle's 'regenerate' runbook step, or a developer preview) silently destroys hand-curated prose. The AC's 'running twice produces no diff' is inconsistent with a file that also contains curated content unless the invocation is scoped (e.g., `--unreleased --prepend`) so only the new/unreleased section is prepended and committed sections are never touched. The plan never names a scoping flag or merge strategy.
findings.location: AC line 2 — 'regeneration is deterministic (running twice produces no diff)'; What section — 'hand-curate the current cycle's top section'; docs/releasing.md step description — 'regenerate with git-cliff, then a ~5-minute curation pass'
findings.suggested_fix: In docs/releasing.md runbook step, replace 'regenerate with git-cliff' with the explicit command `git-cliff --unreleased --prepend CHANGELOG.md`. In cliff.toml, document the expected invocation. Update AC2 to: 'Running `git-cliff --unreleased` twice against the same commit range produces no diff; previously committed and curated sections are never modified by re-generation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the idempotency conflict: specify whether git-cliff is invoked with `--unreleased` (or equivalent scope flag) to protect already-committed sections, or document a protected-header/merge pattern so re-running never overwrites curated content.
findings.finding: The AC requires deterministic regeneration ('running twice produces no diff'), but the plan also mandates hand-curation of the top section before each release. After curation, any re-run of git-cliff targeting the full file will overwrite the curated prose, making writes non-idempotent and potentially corrupting the curated content.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'cliff.toml maps conventional types … regeneration is deterministic (running twice produces no diff)'; What section — 'hand-curate the current cycle's top section'
findings.suggested_fix: In cliff.toml and the docs/releasing.md runbook, mandate `git-cliff --unreleased --prepend CHANGELOG.md` (git-cliff's prepend mode) rather than full-file regeneration. Update the AC to read: 'running git-cliff --unreleased twice on the same commit produces no diff' — scoping the determinism claim to the unreleased window only, not the full file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reframe the `[project.urls]` Changelog step as an **update** (not just verify): the current value `docs/release-notes.md` must be changed to `CHANGELOG.md`, and AC-1 or a new AC should require the URL to point at the new file.
findings.finding: The plan asserts '[project.urls] Changelog already exists in pyproject — verify target' as if the URL may already point to CHANGELOG.md, but the codebase shows it points to `docs/release-notes.md`. The Scope frames this as a 'verify' step, not an update step — meaning the required URL repoint from `docs/release-notes.md` to `CHANGELOG.md` may be treated as optional/out-of-scope when it is actually a required deliverable.
findings.location: What section (fourth bullet) and Scope: 'pyproject urls verification'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What bullet, change 'verify target' to 'update to point at CHANGELOG.md (currently points at docs/release-notes.md — needs repoint)'. Add an AC: `[project.urls] Changelog in pyproject.toml points at CHANGELOG.md after the change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly list `pyproject.toml` as an edit target in the Scope IN list and reword the What bullet from 'verify target' to 'update `[project.urls] Changelog` to point at `CHANGELOG.md`'.
findings.finding: pyproject.toml is a confirmed required edit (the [project.urls] Changelog key currently points at docs/release-notes.md, not CHANGELOG.md) but it is absent from the Scope section's IN list and is described only as 'verify target' in the What section — understating it as a verification step rather than a required edit.
findings.location: Scope section (IN list) / What section bullet 4 / pyproject.toml line 78
findings.suggested_fix: Change the What bullet to: 'Update `[project.urls] Changelog` in `pyproject.toml` to `https://github.com/navapbc/rebar/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md`.' Add `pyproject.toml` to the Scope IN list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `filter_unconventional` belongs under `[git]` in cliff.toml by running `git cliff --generate-config` or checking the cliff.toml schema, and make the plan's cliff.toml snippet explicit about section placement.
findings.finding: The plan cites `filter_unconventional = false` as a specific cliff.toml key but no flag-level empirical verification is recorded. In git-cliff's actual schema the key lives under `[git]` as `filter_unconventional`; the plan's flat reference omits the section context. If the author or executor places it at the top level of cliff.toml it will be silently ignored, causing non-conventional commits to be filtered out — the opposite of the stated intent for mixed-history repos.
findings.location: What section, cliff.toml bullet: 'filter_unconventional = false with a catch-all parser'
findings.suggested_fix: In the cliff.toml spec, make the section explicit: `[git]\nfilter_unconventional = false` so the executor has an unambiguous placement target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC covering `[project.urls]` Changelog verification (e.g. 'The `[project.urls].Changelog` entry in `pyproject.toml` resolves to the committed `CHANGELOG.md`').
findings.finding: The described deliverable 'Check [project.urls] Changelog points at the committed file' (in What and Scope) has no corresponding acceptance criterion — it is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: What section (4th bullet); Scope (IN list); Acceptance Criteria (none)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] `pyproject.toml` `[project.urls]` Changelog entry points to the committed `CHANGELOG.md` (verified by inspection).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the minimum git-cliff version required (e.g. `>=2.x`), add it to the dev toolchain (pyproject.toml `[dev]` or a pre-commit hook), and verify `filter_unconventional` is the correct config key name in that version — cross-reference against the git-cliff changelog/migration guide for any renames between v1 and v2.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `filter_unconventional = false` as a git-cliff config key to handle repos with imperfect commit hygiene, but does not pin a git-cliff version, and the config schema for this key has changed across git-cliff releases (it was restructured in the v2 series). Additionally, git-cliff is not declared anywhere in pyproject.toml dev dependencies, pre-commit config, or the CI workflow, so the specific behavior of this assertion — that a catch-all parser plus `filter_unconventional = false` silently absorbs mixed-history commits rather than erroring — cannot be verified against a known version.
findings.location: What section — cliff.toml bullet: '`filter_unconventional = false` with a catch-all parser for mixed history (per the documented git-cliff migration pattern for repos with imperfect commit hygiene)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `git-cliff>=2.0` to `[project.optional-dependencies.dev]` in pyproject.toml, and in the plan's cliff.toml bullet explicitly note the target version. Confirm via https://git-cliff.org/docs/configuration that `filter_unconventional` (under `[git]`) is the current key name; if it was renamed (e.g. to `filter_commits`), update accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the git-cliff installation method in `docs/releasing.md` (e.g. `pipx install git-cliff`, `cargo install git-cliff`, or add it to `pyproject.toml [dev]`), so the runbook is self-contained for a new maintainer.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies how git-cliff is installed for the maintainer running the release runbook. It is absent from pyproject.toml [dev] dependencies, .pre-commit-config.yaml, Makefile, and all CI workflows — yet the plan adds a release step that requires it. This is an unstated environment precondition: a new maintainer following docs/releasing.md will hit 'command not found' with no guidance.
findings.location: What section ('Wire into docs/releasing.md') and Acceptance Criteria AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add git-cliff to the [dev] extra in pyproject.toml (e.g. `"git-cliff>=2"`) OR document a one-liner in releasing.md such as `pipx install git-cliff` under a 'Prerequisites' subsection. Either makes the install path explicit and auditable.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'per the table above' in AC2 with a direct reference to the What section's prose list, or format the mapping as an actual table in the plan so the AC reference resolves unambiguously.
findings.finding: AC2 references 'the table above' for the commit_parsers mapping, but no table exists in the plan — only a prose inline list. The verify step cannot unambiguously confirm correct mapping without a canonical table.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'cliff.toml maps conventional types per the table above'
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC2 to: 'cliff.toml maps conventional types as specified in the What section (feat→Added, fix→Fixed, breaking→highlighted, perf/refactor→Changed, docs→Documentation; build/ci/chore/test dropped) and regeneration is deterministic.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the expected `[project.urls]` Changelog value explicitly (e.g., `https://github.com/ORG/rebar/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md`) so the executor can verify-and-fix without clarification.
findings.finding: The 'Check [project.urls] Changelog points at the committed file — verify target' bullet in What is a placeholder that defers a real decision to the executor: it says 'verify target' without stating what the correct target URL should be. If the URL is wrong, the executor has no specified correct value to use.
findings.location: What section, fourth bullet: 'Check `[project.urls]` Changelog points at the committed file (a Changelog URL already exists in pyproject — verify target).'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'verify target' with a decision: 'Ensure `[project.urls] Changelog` points to `https://github.com/<org>/rebar/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md`; update if it does not.' Add a corresponding AC: '`[project.urls] Changelog` in pyproject.toml points to the committed CHANGELOG.md on the default branch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan (or docs/releasing.md) that CHANGELOG.md edits will be classified as a 'hard-to-reverse surface' by the code-review impact scorer (`workflow_ops.py`), so changelog curation commits expectedly trigger elevated impact scores.
findings.finding: src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py contains `"CHANGELOG" in base` (line 310) which will permanently classify every future edit to CHANGELOG.md as a 'hard-to-reverse surface', raising the impact score for routine changelog curation commits. This is an unenumerated external consumer of the new file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicit 'out-of-scope with rationale') for the GitHub Releases non-regression check so executors know whether to verify and what 'pass' looks like.
findings.finding: No AC or step captures a non-regression check for GitHub Releases auto-generation. The What section mentions 'Verify the release workflow / auto-generated GitHub Releases notes remain unchanged' but this is a verify-without-stated-outcome bullet and has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion, so it can be silently skipped.
findings.location: What section, fifth bullet ('Verify the release workflow...') and Acceptance Criteria section (no matching AC).
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[ ] The existing GitHub Actions release workflow (`.github/workflows/release.yml` or equivalent) is unchanged and CI passes on a dry-run or review of the workflow file.' Alternatively, move it explicitly to OUT scope with a one-line rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit proving command for the determinism AC, e.g.: `git cliff --output CHANGELOG.md && git cliff --output CHANGELOG2.md && diff CHANGELOG.md CHANGELOG2.md` (or equivalent idempotent re-run check like `git diff --exit-code` after a second `git cliff -o CHANGELOG.md`).
findings.finding: AC2 states regeneration is 'deterministic (running twice produces no diff)' but provides no proving command; without an anchored command the claim has no executable evidence and the executor must improvise a verification step.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'regeneration is deterministic (running twice produces no diff)'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC2: 'Proving command: `git cliff -o CHANGELOG.md && git cliff -o CHANGELOG.md && git diff --exit-code CHANGELOG.md` exits 0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC confirming the GitHub Actions release workflow and auto-generated release notes are unchanged (e.g. 'No changes to `.github/workflows/release.yml` or equivalent; GitHub Releases notes generation is unaffected').
findings.finding: The described step 'Verify the release workflow / auto-generated GitHub Releases notes remain unchanged' (What section) has no corresponding acceptance criterion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC4's location: either change 'on the ticket' to 'in the Why section of this plan' (if the plan is the record), or explicitly commit to recording rationales in the linked issue.
findings.finding: AC4 says rejected alternatives are 'recorded with one-line rationales on the ticket,' but the plan body already records them inline in the Why section — the location is inconsistent, and 'on the ticket' implies an issue tracker rather than the plan document itself.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4 vs Why section
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC4 to: 'Rejected alternatives (towncrier, PR-label generation, GitHub-Releases-only) recorded with one-line rationales in the Why section of this plan (already present).', and consider whether AC4 is still needed as a verifiable criterion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Persist the git-cliff adoption rationale and rejected-alternative one-liners in a committed file (e.g., a 'Decision' subsection in `docs/releasing.md`, a `docs/decisions/changelog-tooling.md`, or `CLAUDE.md`) rather than only on the ticket.
findings.finding: The architectural decision to adopt git-cliff (and the one-line rationales for rejecting towncrier, PR-label generation, and GitHub-Releases-only) is recorded only 'on the ticket' — an ephemeral, out-of-repo location — rather than in a committed file (e.g., docs/decisions/changelog-tooling.md, CLAUDE.md, or a Why section inside docs/releasing.md).
findings.location: AC line 4: 'Rejected alternatives … recorded with one-line rationales on the ticket'; Why section
findings.suggested_fix: Append a short 'Decision rationale' block to `docs/releasing.md` (already planned for editing) that includes the one-liner for each rejected alternative (towncrier, PR-label generation, GitHub-Releases-only) and the 'generate-then-curate dial position' the plan already articulates.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the actual conventional-commit adoption rate in the git history (e.g., `git log --oneline | grep -cE '^[a-f0-9]+ (feat|fix|build|chore|ci|docs|perf|refactor|test)(!)?[:(]'`) and either add a pre-commit conventional-commit hook to make the 'already pays for' claim accurate, or acknowledge in the plan that historical coverage will rely on `filter_unconventional = false` catch-all.
findings.finding: The plan's central justification — 'the conventional-commit discipline rebar already pays for (`feat:`/`fix:`/`build:` subjects)' — is stated as an existing, enforced practice, but no enforcement mechanism exists in the codebase. The `.pre-commit-config.yaml` has no commitlint or conventional-commit hook, and no other tooling enforces the format. The only `feat:` occurrences are in test-fixture strings. If commits in practice are not conventional, git-cliff will emit a sparse/empty changelog relying on the `filter_unconventional = false` catch-all.
findings.location: Why section: 'the conventional-commit discipline rebar already pays for (`feat:`/`fix:`/`build:` subjects)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
